questions.question: Define a Linked List and explain why it is called a dynamic data structure.
keyPoints: A linked list is a linear data structure with nodes containing data and pointers
It is called dynamic because memory is allocated at runtime
Size can change during execution unlike fixed-size arrays
No memory wastage as nodes can be created/destroyed as needed

questions.question: Differentiate between Singly Linked List and Doubly Linked List.
keyPoints: Singly has one pointer (next) per node; Doubly has two (prev and next)
Singly allows unidirectional traversal; Doubly allows bidirectional
Doubly uses more memory per node
Deletion of previous node is easier in Doubly linked list

questions.question: Write the algorithm to insert a node at the beginning of a Singly Linked List.
keyPoints: Create a new node with given data
Set new node's next pointer to current head
Update head pointer to point to new node
Time complexity is O(1)

questions.question: Explain the traversal operation in a Linked List with an example.
keyPoints: Traversal visits each node exactly once starting from head
Use a temporary pointer initialized to head
Move pointer to next node until NULL is reached
Can count nodes, print data, or search for elements during traversal

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Linked Lists over Arrays?
keyPoints: Advantages: Dynamic size, efficient insertion/deletion at beginning, no memory wastage
Disadvantages: No random access, more memory per element, not cache-friendly
Arrays provide O(1) access but fixed size
Choice depends on specific application requirements

questions.question: What is a Circular Linked List? Explain with a suitable example.
keyPoints: In circular linked list, last node points back to first node
Can be singly or doubly circular
No NULL pointer at the end
Used in round-robin scheduling, circular buffers

questions.question: Write an algorithm to delete a node from the end of a Singly Linked List.
keyPoints: Handle empty list and single node cases
Traverse to find second-last node
Set second-last node's next to NULL
Free the memory of deleted node
Time complexity is O(n)

questions.question: Explain the process of insertion and deletion operations in a Linked List. Write algorithms for inserting a node after a given node and deleting a node with a specific value.
keyPoints: Insertion: Create node, update pointers to maintain links
Deletion: Find node, adjust previous node's pointer, free memory
Insert after node: Set new->next = prev->next, then prev->next = new
Delete with value: Traverse to find node, handle edge cases for head deletion
Both operations require proper pointer handling to avoid memory leaks

questions.question: Compare Array and Linked List data structures. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Arrays: Contiguous memory, O(1) random access, fixed size, cache-friendly
Linked Lists: Non-contiguous, O(n) access, dynamic size, extra pointer memory
Choose Array when: Size known, frequent random access needed, memory locality important
Choose Linked List when: Dynamic sizing, frequent insertions/deletions at beginning, unpredictable size
Hybrid approaches exist for specific use cases

questions.question: Write a complete C/Python program to implement a Singly Linked List with functions for creation, insertion (at beginning, end, and middle), deletion, and traversal.
keyPoints: Define Node structure with data and next pointer
Implement createNode function
Insert at beginning: O(1), at end: O(n), at position: O(n)
Delete from beginning, end, or by value
Traverse to display all elements
Handle all edge cases properly
